name: Sila
description: Sila, also known as Silap Inua, is a central figure in Inuit mythology, representing the breath of life, the sky, the weather, and the spirit that permeates all things. Sila is not typically depicted in anthropomorphic form, but rather as an all-encompassing force. It controls the wind, rain, snow, and storms, and therefore has immense power over the lives of humans and animals. Sila is considered both benevolent and malevolent; it provides the air needed to breathe and sustains life, but also unleashes harsh weather that can bring hardship and death. Respect and appeasement of Sila are crucial for survival in the Arctic environment. Inuit shamans (Angakkuq) communicate with Sila to understand the causes of misfortune and to restore balance in the world. Sila's influence extends beyond the physical realm, encompassing the moral and spiritual well-being of the community. Transgressions against the natural world or social norms can anger Sila, leading to negative consequences. Maintaining harmony with Sila requires adherence to traditional beliefs and practices, demonstrating respect for the environment and all living creatures. Ultimately, Sila embodies the interconnectedness of all things and the delicate balance between life and death in the Arctic landscape.
subtitle: Breath of life, spirit of the world

keyMyths.title: Appeasing Sila for a Good Hunt
keyMyths.description: A community performs rituals to appease Sila, ensuring favorable weather conditions for a successful seal hunt. Their respect and offerings bring them sustenance.

keyMyths.title: The Angakkuq's Journey to Sila
keyMyths.description: An Angakkuq undertakes a spiritual journey to communicate with Sila, seeking guidance to resolve a community crisis. They learn of the community's wrongdoings and how to atone for them.

keyMyths.title: Sila's Wrath and the Great Storm
keyMyths.description: A village disregards traditional customs, angering Sila, who unleashes a devastating storm. Only through repentance and renewed respect for nature can the village restore balance.

sources.author: Franz Boas
